content.problemStatement: Johnny has reached the final trial on the Path of Doom. This is the hardest one. He entered a large room. The floor of the room is divided into square cells of the same size. There are n rows and m columns of cells. Each cell can be either red or blue. We can consider that the walls of the room are parallel south-north direction and west-east direction and that Johnny has entered the room in the north-western corner. So now he is standing in the north-western cell of the room. The exit is in the south-eastern corner. Johnny can make steps in each of the eight directions moving to adjacent cells. He has to reach the exit. But when he goes out of the room all the cells in the room should be blue, otherwise he fails the trial (If Johnny goes out he can't step back again). The colors of the cells change after each step Johnny makes. When he steps on the next cell this cell and the cells to its north, south, east and west change color: from blue to red and from red to blue. Johnny can't ever step on two cells at once or he fails the trial. Also just jumping on the cell he is standing on now will have no effect on the color of cells. Currently some cells in the room may be red. Johnny made a look over the room and he wonders if it's even possible to pass the trial at all or the trial master is playing a trick on him. Help him find this out.
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- A line with two integers n and m (1 ≤ n, m ≤ 10^9, min(n,m) < 40).
- A line with integer k (0 ≤ k ≤ min(n·m, 10000)), the number of red cells.
- k lines follow, each with two integers x and y (1‑based coordinates of a red cell).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if Johnny can finish the trial, otherwise "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: First case: 0 red cells (even) → YES. Second case: 4 red cells (even) → YES. Third case: 1 red cell (odd) → NO.
No red cells, parity is even, so Johnny can finish.
Five red cells – odd parity – impossible to make all cells blue.

solution.approach: Each move toggles the colour of the stepped‑on cell's north, south, east and west neighbours. The cell where Johnny stands does not change. This operation flips the colour of exactly four cells (or fewer on the border). Therefore the parity (even/odd) of the total number of red cells never changes during the whole walk. To leave the room all cells must be blue, i.e. the number of red cells must become zero, which is possible only if the initial number of red cells is even. Hence the answer is YES iff k is even.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T + total_k) – we only read the input and check k%2, no heavy computation.

code.Python3:
import sys
def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        n = int(next(it))
        m = int(next(it))
        k = int(next(it))
        for __ in range(k):
            next(it); next(it)  # skip coordinates
        out.append('YES' if k % 2 == 0 else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
